content.text: Time-to-first-deploy is the end-to-end elapsed time from when a team starts using the GenAI platform to when they serve their first production request through it. It is the platform's equivalent of a startup's time-to-first-value metric: it captures the combined friction of onboarding, credential provisioning, integration, evaluation, and deployment in a single number. Platform teams that track this metric can identify exactly where teams get stuck, measure the impact of documentation improvements, and set meaningful SLOs for the onboarding experience.

Time-to-first-deploy is not a single event — it is a funnel of observable milestones, each of which can be instrumented independently. Tracking each milestone separately reveals where drop-off happens, not just how long the total journey takes.
First successful import of genai-sdk in any environment
Backstage provisioning form submitted and key delivered
HTTP 200 from LLM gateway with non-zero output tokens
Eval runner job completes for this team's service
First gateway request with production environment tag
M4→M5 gap = CI/CD or approval friction

Each milestone is recorded as a structured event in the platform's event store. Events are keyed on the team identifier (from the API key metadata), making it possible to trace the complete journey for any team.

# platform_events.py — event schema for milestone tracking

from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
import json, httpx

EVENT_STORE = "https://events.internal/api/platform-milestones"

@dataclass
class MilestoneEvent:
    team_id:     str
    service_id:  str
    milestone:   str        # M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5
    environment: str        # dev | staging | production
    sdk_version: str | None = None
    model:       str | None = None
    metadata:    dict       = None

    def emit(self) -> None:
        payload = asdict(self)
        payload["occurred_at"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
        httpx.post(EVENT_STORE, json=payload, timeout=2.0)

# LLM gateway emits M3 on first successful call:
# MilestoneEvent(
#     team_id="team:payments",
#     service_id="payment-copilot",
#     milestone="M3",
#     environment="dev",
#     model="gpt-4o-mini",
# ).emit()

# Provisioning service emits M2 on key delivery:
# MilestoneEvent(
#     team_id="team:payments",
#     service_id="payment-copilot",
#     milestone="M2",
#     environment="dev",
# ).emit()

Once baselines are established, the platform team sets SLOs for each milestone delta. SLOs make the metric actionable: a P75 that exceeds the SLO triggers a quarterly investigation, and sustained SLO breaches block platform team capacity from new features until the onboarding experience is fixed.
Review provisioning service logs; check for approval queue backlog
Audit onboarding doc quality; review error logs for common SDK errors
Review CI/CD pipeline; check eval runner capacity; survey blocked teams
Overall onboarding health; inspect funnel drop-off rates by step

Median times look healthy when a fast minority pulls the average down. The P75 (75th percentile) captures the experience of the team that is struggling — not the team that sailed through. A P75 SLO means 75% of teams must meet the target, which is a meaningful bar without being punishingly strict.

The funnel diagram above shows a common pattern: the largest single drop-off is between M2 (API key provisioned) and M3 (first successful LLM call). This means teams have credentials but cannot make a working call — the friction is in SDK usage, documentation, or credential injection, not in the provisioning process itself.

# diagnose_m2_m3_dropoff.py
# Find teams stuck between M2 and M3 for > 48 hours

import httpx
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

events = get_events("2026-04-01", "2026-06-30")
team_milestones = defaultdict(dict)
for ev in events:
    tid, ms, ts = ev["team_id"], ev["milestone"], datetime.fromisoformat(ev["occurred_at"])
    if ms not in team_milestones[tid]:
        team_milestones[tid][ms] = ts

cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=48)
stuck_teams = []

for tid, ms in team_milestones.items():
    if "M2" in ms and "M3" not in ms:
        hours_since_m2 = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - ms["M2"]).total_seconds() / 3600
        if hours_since_m2 > 48:
            stuck_teams.append({"team": tid, "hours_since_m2": round(hours_since_m2, 1)})

stuck_teams.sort(key=lambda x: x["hours_since_m2"], reverse=True)
print(f"{len(stuck_teams)} teams stuck between M2 and M3:")
for t in stuck_teams[:10]:
    print(f"  {t['team']:30s}: {t['hours_since_m2']}h since key provisioned")

# Automated action: send a proactive support message to stuck teams via Slack

Teams identified as stuck between M2 and M3 receive a proactive Slack message from the platform team: not a survey, but a direct offer of help — 'We noticed your team provisioned an API key 48 hours ago but hasn't made a successful call yet. Can we jump on a 15-minute call?' This converts a silent drop-off into a support interaction that also yields qualitative data about what went wrong.
Cohort Analysis: Platform Improvements Over Time

Cohort analysis compares the funnel metrics for teams that started in different quarters, allowing the platform team to measure whether improvements (better docs, faster provisioning, clearer error messages) actually reduced time-to-first-deploy.

# cohort_analysis.py — compare T2FD across quarterly cohorts

import httpx, statistics
from datetime import datetime

def cohort_label(ts: datetime) -> str:
    return f"Q{(ts.month - 1) // 3 + 1} {ts.year}"

events = get_events("2025-01-01", "2026-06-30")
cohort_deploys: dict[str, list[float]] = defaultdict(list)

team_milestones = defaultdict(dict)
for ev in events:
    tid, ms, ts = ev["team_id"], ev["milestone"], datetime.fromisoformat(ev["occurred_at"])
    if ms not in team_milestones[tid]:
        team_milestones[tid][ms] = ts

for tid, ms in team_milestones.items():
    if "M1" not in ms or "M5" not in ms:
        continue
    cohort = cohort_label(ms["M1"])
    hours  = (ms["M5"] - ms["M1"]).total_seconds() / 3600
    cohort_deploys[cohort].append(hours)

print("Cohort       | Median T2FD | P75 T2FD | Teams")
print("-------------|-------------|----------|------")
for cohort in sorted(cohort_deploys.keys()):
    vals = sorted(cohort_deploys[cohort])
    med  = statistics.median(vals)
    p75  = vals[int(len(vals) * 0.75)]
    print(f"{cohort:12s} | {med:>9.0f}h | {p75:>6.0f}h | {len(vals)}")
A cohort analysis from a representative platform might show:
SDK error messages link to docs; proactive stuck-team outreach
In-IDE starter template; eval runner fast-path for < 50 rows

The metric needs to be reported in terms stakeholders can act on. Engineering leadership cares about the trend and whether teams are blocked; product owners care about whether the platform investment is accelerating feature delivery.

# Q2 2026 Platform Health Report — Time-to-First-Deploy Summary

**Metric:** Median time from SDK install to first production GenAI request

| Quarter | Median | P75   | Teams shipped | Change  |
|---------|--------|-------|---------------|---------|
| Q1 2026 | 24h    | 48h   | 56 teams      | —       |
| Q2 2026 | 18h    | 36h   | 71 teams      | -25% ✅ |

**SLO status:** P75 = 36h vs target 48h. ✅ Meeting SLO.

**Funnel drop-off this quarter:**
- M1 → M2 (provisioning): 94% conversion (up from 78% in Q1)
- M2 → M3 (first call): 82% conversion (up from 61% in Q1)
- M3 → M5 (deploy): 72% conversion (stable)

**Root cause of remaining M2→M3 drop-off:**
Proactive outreach identified 8 stuck teams. 6/8 blocked on Vault agent
sidecar annotation syntax (fixed: added annotated example to onboarding guide).

**Investment driving improvement:**
- In-IDE starter template (2 engineer-weeks)
- Proactive stuck-team Slack bot (3 engineer-days)
- Eval runner fast-path for small datasets (1 engineer-week)

Rather than waiting for stuck teams to file a support ticket, the platform runs an automated Slack bot that identifies and reaches out to teams that have stalled between milestones. The bot checks daily, compares the current milestone for every active team against the expected time window, and sends a single targeted message — not a generic reminder, but a message that names the specific milestone where they are stuck.

# bot/stuck_team_outreach.py
# Run nightly as a Kubernetes CronJob

import httpx, os
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

SLACK_WEBHOOK = os.environ["SLACK_PLATFORM_BOT_WEBHOOK"]

def send_outreach(team_id: str, milestone: str, hours_stuck: float) -> None:
    stage_messages = {
        "M2": (
            "provisioned an API key but haven't made a successful LLM call yet. "
            "Common blockers: Vault sidecar annotation, wrong gateway URL, or "
            "missing model-tier permission. "
            "Check the troubleshooting guide: https://wiki.internal/genai/troubleshoot-m3"
        ),
        "M3": (
            "made your first LLM call but haven't run an eval yet. "
            "Run `genai-eval run submit --dataset sample:1.0 --models gpt-4o-mini` "
            "to get your first eval score in < 5 minutes."
        ),
        "M4": (
            "completed an eval run but haven't deployed to production yet. "
            "The most common blocker is the CI eval gate threshold. "
            "Set `threshold: 0` in the eval action to compare-only mode while iterating."
        ),
    }
    if milestone not in stage_messages:
        return

    message = (
        f":wave: Hey {team_id}! The platform team noticed you {stage_messages[milestone]} "
        f"You've been at this stage for {hours_stuck:.0f} hours. "
        f"Can we help? Reply here or ping #platform-support."
    )
    httpx.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={"text": message, "channel": f"@{team_id}"})

# Main loop: find stuck teams and send outreach
events = get_events(
    (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(days=30)).isoformat(),
    datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
)
team_milestones = build_team_milestones(events)

THRESHOLDS = {"M2": 48, "M3": 96, "M4": 120}   # hours before outreach triggers

for tid, ms in team_milestones.items():
    last_milestone = max(ms.keys())
    if last_milestone == "M5":
        continue   # fully deployed, no outreach needed
    hours_since = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - ms[last_milestone]).total_seconds() / 3600
    threshold   = THRESHOLDS.get(last_milestone, 999)
    if hours_since > threshold:
        send_outreach(tid, last_milestone, hours_since)
Teams appear to 'deploy' in CI but never serve real traffic; T2FD looks short but platform adoption is low
Filter M5 events to requests where env tag = 'production' and input_tokens > 0 (real traffic, not test probes)
Measuring from first repo commit, not first SDK install
T2FD includes time spent on product decisions before platform adoption starts; metric is noisy and inflated
Use M1 (first SDK import) as the clock start — it measures the platform friction, not business decision speed
Platform team cannot detect SDK installs that never progress; silent drop-off before M1 is invisible
Make opt-in telemetry the default in the Backstage golden-path template; document clearly in the SDK README
Treating all teams as one cohort
A team migrating an existing service has very different T2FD than a greenfield team; averages are misleading
Segment by 'new service' vs 'migration' and report separately; SLOs can differ between segments
SLO based on mean rather than percentile
A few very fast teams pull the mean down; 30% of teams are above SLO but the mean looks green
Set SLOs on P75 (or P90 for critical onboarding paths); means hide the long tail that matters most

Time-to-first GenAI deploy is the platform team's primary onboarding health metric. Decomposed into five observable milestones — SDK install, API key, first LLM call, eval run, and production deploy — it reveals exactly where teams get stuck rather than just measuring an aggregate delay. Automated event emission from the gateway and provisioning service makes the funnel observable without requiring teams to self-report. SLOs at the P75 quantile hold the team accountable to the experience of struggling teams, not the average. Cohort analysis makes the impact of documentation and tooling improvements measurable. And proactive stuck-team outreach converts silent drop-offs into both support interactions and qualitative data that drives the next improvement.
